title: The Clipper Races: an era of competition between cargo ships

passages.title: The Clipper Races: an era of competition between cargo ships
passages.source_html:
The Clipper Races: an era of competition between cargo ships


A 


During the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries, the British East India Company had the monopoly on trade with China and India. This meant that because no rival could legally import tea or other goods from these countries at this time, the company was rarely in a hurry to transport its merchandise. Instead, its priority was to minimise costs by carrying as much as possible on each ship. This meant that its ships – known as East Indiamen – were enormous, strong and very slow.


B 


By 1800, the average East Indiaman could carry 1,200 tons of merchandise. The trading pattern for China tea usually meant the East Indiamen set sail from Britain in January, sailed round the Cape of Good Hope at the southernmost tip of Africa, and arrived in China in September. There they would load up that year's tea harvest, set off again and, depending on the wind and weather, aim to arrive back by the following September, so even with favourable sailing conditions, the round trip lasted almost two years, and if anything went wrong it could take lot longer.


C 


However, by 1834 the company had lost its trading monopoles, and tea had become a freely traded item. Having no more use for its great ships, the company sold them off, and many were bought by merchants or their captains, who continued to plough the seas between Britain and China. But now that tea could be traded freely, a few smart sailors began to realise that whoever brought each new harvest of tea to Britain first, stood to make the most money.


D 


This was partly because if you were home first, you could sell your shipment of tea before your competitors even arrived, and partly because consumers in Britain in the nineteenth century believed that the fresher and earlier-picked the tea, the better the resulting drink. Tea traders now needed faster, sleeker ships to bring their precious cargo back. Nevertheless, in Britain this idea only caught on slowly, and while the 1840s saw a few faster ships launched, for the time being many merchants remained satisfied with the slow but reliable East Indiamen.


E 


In fact it was the Americans who pioneered the first clipper ships. These vessels were fast and slender, with a narrow hull that was deeper at the back than at the front, and masses of sails on tall masts. They earned their name from the way that they 'clipped off' journey time. British merchants resolved to build their own clippers to rival the Americans, and the first British tea clipper, Shinyway, was built in Aberdeen in 1850. More tea clippers were designed and built in Britain throughout the 1850s and 1860s; they had a narrower beam than their American equivalents, making them less powerful during storms, but faster in calmer weather.


F 


There was a great spirit of competition between the British and American ships plying the tea trade, but to begin with the Americans had the edge. Then in 1851 a British ship owner, Richard Green, built the aptly named clipper Challenger with the stated intention of beating the American ships. Loaded with tea, Challenger left China for London in 1852 at the same time as the American clipper Challenge, a much larger older ship, already greatly admired for its speed. Large sums were bet on which would complete the journey first. In the event, the British ship beat its rival to London by two days, amid much jubilation. From then on, such international races grew in popularity.


G 


After 1855, American participation in the British tea trade gradually stopped. But even without the Anglo-American rivalry, the competitive spirit continued. It was really ignited when new ports were opened up for trade in China. These included Fouchow, which was much closer to the tea-producing areas than Canton, the port used previously. As a result, tea could be loaded on board earlier and fresher, and the clippers could set off in late May or early June – sometimes not even taking time to fill out the official paperwork – racing back to Britain whatever the difficulties.


H 


They sped down through the South China Sea and into the Indian Ocean, then raced to get round the southernmost tip of Africa at the Cape of Good Hope. Then it was north across the vast Atlantic, past the Azores, through the English Channel and into the estuary of the River Thames. Once there, they would be towed by tugs, up the river and into the docks.


I


The cargo of the winning ship could earn premium of up to sixpence per pound – and so the captain and crew were rewarded by the owners of the cargo. But the races were about more than just money: the crews, about 40 men on each clipper, were expert sailors, proud of their ships, and they delighted in competing against each other. Without their enthusiasm, the races would never have happened, since home as fast as possible required the crew to be totally dedicated and to sacrifice much of their rest for the duration of the race.

groups.title: Questions 1-13
groups.source_html:
Questions 1 - 6


Choose TRUE if the statement agrees with the information given in the text, choose FALSE if the statement contradicts the information, or choose NOT GIVEN if there is no information on this.

1

In the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries, the British East India Company faced a lot of competition.

正确答案： B. FALSE

审题：题目询问英国东印度公司在17和18世纪是否面临大量竞争。


定位关键信息： “seventeenth and eighteenth centuries”、“competition”。


对应原文：A段，“During the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries, the British East India Company had the monopoly on trade with China and India. This meant that because no rival could legally import tea or other goods from these countries at this time, the company was rarely in a hurry to transport its merchandise.”


解题思路：A段指出，英国东印度公司对与中国和印度的贸易拥有垄断权，并且当时没有竞争对手可以合法地从这些国家进口茶叶或其他商品，因此说明它并没有面临竞争。


2

Before 1800, cargo size was the most important consideration for the East India Company.

正确答案： B. FALSE

审题：题目询问1800年前东印度公司最重要的考虑是否是货物的大小。


定位关键信息： “before 1800”、“cargo size”。


对应原文：A段，“ Instead, its priority was to minimise costs by carrying as much as possible on each ship. ”


解题思路：A段指出公司优先考虑的是减少成本，而尽可能多地携带货物是实现这一目的的方法，而不是优先考虑货物的大小、尺寸。


3

At best, voyages of the East Indiamen to China and back took nearly two years to complete.

正确答案： A. TRUE

审题：题目询问东印度公司的船到中国往返是否需要将近两年。


定位关键信息： “two years”、“to China and back”。


对应原文：B段，“The trading pattern for China tea usually meant the East Indiamen set sail from Britain in January, sailed round the Cape of Good Hope at the southernmost tip of Africa, and arrived in China in September. There they would load up that year's tea harvest, set off again and, depending on the wind and weather, aim to arrive back by the following September, so even with favourable sailing conditions, the round trip lasted almost two years, and if anything went wrong it could take lot longer.”


解题思路：B段指出在航行条件最好的情况下，往返需要将近两年。如果如果出了什么差错，可能要花更长的时间。


同义替换：nearly = almost 

4

Before 1834, voyages to and from China were considered to be highly dangerous.

正确答案： C. NOT GIVEN

审题：题目询问1834年之前，往返中国的航行是否被认为非常危险。


定位关键信息： “before 1834”、“dangerous”。


对应原文：C段，However, by 1834 the company had lost its trading monopoles, and tea had become a freely traded item.


解题思路：C段指出到1834年，该公司失去了贸易垄断权，茶叶成为一种自由交易的商品，没有相关信息提及航行是否危险。


5

After 1834, the ships which had served the East India Company stopped being used for commercial purposes.

正确答案： B. FALSE

审题：题目询问1834年后，曾服务东印度公司的船是否停止了商业用途。


定位关键信息： “after 1834”、“stopped being used for commercial purposes”。


对应原文：C段，“Having no more use for its great ships, the company sold them off, and many were bought by merchants or their captains, who continued to plough the seas between Britain and China.”


解题思路：C段指出这些船在被商人购买后继续进行贸易。


6

In the nineteen century, British drinkers preferred tea made from mature leaves to that made from younger leaves.

正确答案： B. FALSE

审题：题目询问19世纪时英国饮茶者是否更喜欢用成熟叶子制成的茶。


定位关键信息： “nineteen century”、“preferred”、“mature leaves”。


对应原文：D段，“This was partly because if you were home first, you could sell your shipment of tea before your competitors even arrived, and partly because consumers in Britain in the nineteenth century believed that the fresher and earlier-picked the tea, the better the resulting drink.”


解题思路：D段指出英国消费者更喜欢新鲜的、早采摘的茶。

Questions 7 - 13


Complete the notes. Write ONE WORD ONLY in each gap.

7

Clipper races

The ships

• Clipper ships were first used for trading by American merchants.

• The ships were remarkable for the number of  they had.

正确答案： 7 sails
Question 7

定位关键词：remarkable for, number


对应原文：E段，"These vessels were fast and slender, with a narrow hull that was deeper at the back than at the front, and masses of sails on tall masts."


解题思路：E段指出"masses of sails"表示"大量的帆"，说明它们以帆的数量著称。


同义替换：ships=vessels

8

• The performance of British tea clippers was particularly affected when there were  at sea.

正确答案： 8 storms
Question 8

定位关键词：performance, British tea clippers, affected, at sea


对应原文：E段，"More tea clippers were designed and built in Britain throughout the 1850s and 1860s; they had a narrower beam than their American equivalents, making them less powerful during storms, but faster in calmer weather."


解题思路：D段指出，在风暴期间，英国茶船因较窄的船体表现较差。说明风暴（storms）对其表现有影响。


9

The races

• It was in a ship called  that the British first competed successfully against the Americans.

• Richard Green's ship arrived two days ahead of its competitor.

正确答案： 9 Challenger
Question 9

定位关键词：ship, British, first competed successfully


对应原文：F段， "Then in 1851 a British ship owner, Richard Green, built the aptly named clipper Challenger with the stated intention of beating the American ships. "


解题思路：E段指出 Challenger 是为了击败美国船而建造的，并且首次成功。


10

• Competition increased when additional Chinese trading  were established.

正确答案： 10 ports
Question 10

定位关键词：competition increased, Chinese trading


对应原文：G段，"But even without the Anglo-American rivalry, the competitive spirit continued. It was really ignited when new ports were opened up for trade in China."


解题思路：F段指出，当中国的新港口开放后，竞争加剧。


同义替换：established=opened up

11

• Merchants were occasionally in such a hurry that they failed to complete the  before leaving China.

正确答案： 11 paperwork
Question 11

定位关键词：merchants, hurry, failed to complete


对应原文：G段，"As a result, tea could be loaded on board earlier and fresher, and the clippers could set off in late May or early June – sometimes not even taking time to fill out the official paperwork – racing back to Britain whatever the difficulties."


解题思路：G段指出他们有时连官方的文件（paperwork）都没有时间填写。


同义替换：failed to complete=not even taking time to fill out


12

• At the end of their journey, the ships needed the help of .

正确答案： 12 tugs
Question 12

定位关键词：end of journey, ships, help of


对应原文：H段，"Once there, they would be towed by tugs, up the river and into the docks."


解题思路：H段指出船只会被拖船 (tugs) 拖进港口。

13

The rewards

• The crews were motivated by both  and their enthusiasm for the competition.

正确答案： 13 premium##money
Question 13

定位关键词：crews, motivated by, enthusiasm


对应原文：I段，"The cargo of the winning ship could earn premium of up to sixpence per pound – and so the captain and crew were rewarded by the owners of the cargo."


解题思路：I段指出，船员不仅因奖金（premium，也就是钱）和他们对比赛的热情而受到激励。
